explanation: True — 66.7% (2 of 3 authors). On p. 285, the paper lists Ignazio Angeloni and Marco Lo Duca with European Central Bank affiliation, while Ester Faia is listed at Goethe University Frankfurt/CFS/SAFE. External checks confirm Angeloni held Bank of Italy/ECB roles and Lo Duca has worked at the ECB since 2004; Faia's ECB consulting is not counted as central-bank staff employment.

Taxes are only lump-sum transfers: T_t is defined as net transfers to the public sector equal to public expenditures on p. 292, and G_t is financed by lump-sum taxes on p. 297; there is no distortionary tax wedge or tax rule with independent equilibrium effects.

The model has nontrivial lagged state dependence: the household budget constraint includes last period's deposits D_t-1 on p. 292, bank capital accumulates from lagged bank capital on p. 295, and capital accumulation is part of the equilibrium system on p. 297.
Section 5.4 is titled Intermediate good producers and says each intermediate-good firm has monopolistic power over its own variety and faces quadratic price adjustment costs, p. 296.

The model is solved with model-consistent forward-looking expectations: inflation and tightness equations contain E_t terms, and the paper says inflation is forward-looking through dependence on current and future productivity on p. 15.

Lagged terms matter through employment/unemployment dynamics: labor-market tightness depends on current and lagged employment on p. 14, and under strict inflation targeting unemployment follows u_t = b u_t-1 - c gamma a_t, giving intrinsic unemployment persistence on pp. 17-18.

The wage rigidity is imposed directly as a reduced-form real wage schedule W_t = Theta A_t^(1-gamma), where gamma indexes real wage rigidity; it is not Calvo, Rotemberg, wage contracting, or Nash bargaining, though gamma = 0 nests the Nash-bargained wage benchmark and gamma = 1 gives a rigid real wage; see pp. 11-12.

Prior to the 2010 publication, no author had qualifying central-bank employment/officer/staff work under the rule that consultancies do not count. Page 1 lists Olivier Blanchard and Jordi Gali with academic/NBER/CEPR affiliations; the evidence found for Gali was central-bank consulting/visitor/adviser roles rather than central-bank employment, and Blanchard's CV lists MIT/PIIE and IMF roles but no central-bank employment.

The model uses rational/model-consistent expectations: the log-linearized Phillips curve contains E_t{pi_t+1}, and the authors solve the optimal-policy system using standard linear stochastic difference-equation methods; see the forward-looking inflation equation on p. 14 and the optimal-policy solution discussion on p. 19.

The sticky-wage element is reduced-form real wage rigidity, not Calvo/Rotemberg/contracting: on p. 11 they impose W_t = Theta A_t^(1-gamma), with gamma in [0,1] indexing the degree of real wage rigidity.

True — 33.3% (1 of 3 authors) before the 1999 publication under the rule that consultancies do not count. The paper lists Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist on p. 1341; Gilchrist was a Federal Reserve Board staff economist before publication, Bernanke's Fed Governor/Chair roles came after 1999, and Gertler's cited central-bank connection was consulting/advisory work and is not counted.

Taxes appear only as lump-sum taxes in the household budget constraint on p. 1387 and as part of government financing on p. 1389; there is no distortionary tax wedge or tax rule that independently affects equilibrium allocation.

The model is a conventional DNK framework with forward-looking private-sector behavior; on p. 1364 the Phillips curve uses expected future inflation, reflecting the forward-looking nature of price setting, which is the rational-expectations structure rather than adaptive learning.

The model explicitly includes lag structure: the authors state on p. 1342 that they allow for lags in investment, and on p. 1365 they say this lets the model generate hump-shaped output dynamics and a lead-lag relation between asset prices and investment.

Sticky prices apply at the retail/final-goods level: entrepreneurs produce wholesale goods competitively, while retailers differentiate wholesale output, sell final usable goods under monopolistic competition, and face Calvo price adjustment on pp. 1360-1361 and 1388-1389.
It was published as a chapter in the Handbook of Macroeconomics, Volume 1 by Elsevier, not as an academic journal article; the chapter title and handbook publication information appear on p. 1341.

The model is solved under model-consistent/rational expectations: the household Fisher equation and asset-pricing equation are written with E_t, explicitly defined as the expectations operator on p. 41.

The model has nontrivial endogenous lagged state dependence through entrepreneurial equity/net worth: equation (25) has e_t depending on e_t-1, and the authors state that equations (25)-(26) track the dynamic behavior of entrepreneurial wealth and the agency-cost distortion; see p. 46.

The model has lump-sum taxes in the household budget and a steady-state subsidy/tax choice used to offset markup distortions, but there is no government-spending process or G_t term entering goods-market clearing/equilibrium demand; see the household budget on p. 5 and the fiscal-authority subsidy setup on p. 16.

The model is solved under model-consistent forward-looking expectations: households maximize expected discounted utility on p. 5, and the log-linear equilibrium uses E_t expectations in the consumption Euler equation, IS curve, and Phillips curve on pp. 14-15.

The structural IS and Phillips curves are forward-looking, but the model includes a nontrivial lagged state through the cost-push shock process u_t = rho u_t-1 + epsilon_t on p. 15, and that persistence enters the equilibrium solution for inflation/output through psi = [(1 - beta rho) + lambda xi]^-1 on p. 17.
The price-setting block is plain Calvo: firms that cannot reset keep their price fixed, and the Phillips curve is pi_t = delta mc_t + beta E_t pi_t+1, with no lagged-inflation/indexation term.

It was not only a working paper: the uploaded version is NBER Working Paper No. 8870, April 2002 on p. 1, but the paper was also published in the Journal of Monetary Economics, vol. 49, issue 5, pp. 879-904, in 2002.
It was published in an academic journal: Journal of Monetary Economics, vol. 49, issue 5, pp. 879-904, in 2002; the uploaded paper itself is the NBER working-paper version dated April 2002 on p. 1.

The paper is solved analytically rather than calibrated; the authors say the model is sufficiently tractable to solve analytically on p. 1 and later state that a serious model calibration is beyond the scope of the paper on p. 25.

Taxes are not modeled as a nontrivial fiscal instrument or equilibrium channel in the baseline model; they are only mentioned as a possible distortion that could make the welfare-maximizing output level exceed the natural level, not as tax rates entering household/firm conditions or government budget dynamics; see pp. 1665 and 1669.

The paper does not introduce a final/intermediate-goods production chain; sticky pricing comes from monopolistically competitive firms that choose nominal prices subject to staggered adjustment, generating the aggregate Phillips curve; see p. 1666.

The paper presents a stripped-down baseline model to derive policy principles, and Section 2 gives the model analytically rather than assigning numerical parameter values or matching moments; later quantitative discussion concerns estimated policy rules, not calibration of the baseline model. See pp. 1662 and 1664-1667.

The model has lump-sum taxes in the household budget constraint and government budget constraint, but they only finance unemployment benefits, debt, and exogenous government spending; there is no distortionary labor/capital/consumption tax wedge or tax rule that independently affects equilibrium allocations on pp. 868 and 871.

The model includes one-period nominal government bonds in the household budget constraint on p. 868 and the government budget constraint on p. 871, but debt is only a Ricardian financing/asset device with lump-sum taxes; there is no nontrivial public-debt stock, debt feedback rule, maturity structure, fiscal-risk channel, or debt-driven equilibrium effect.

The model has nontrivial lagged state dependence through external habit in consumption on p. 867, employment law of motion using lagged employment and matches, Calvo wage contracts tied to previous wages, and an interest-rate rule with lagged R_t and lagged year-on-year inflation on p. 871.

Sticky prices apply in the wholesale sector, which corresponds to differentiated intermediate-goods producers: on p. 869, wholesale firms face Calvo price-setting frictions, while final-good retailers aggregate wholesale goods competitively.

The sticky wage friction is Calvo-style: only with probability 1 - gamma can a firm-worker pair rebargain its hourly nominal wage in a given period; the reset wage is determined through right-to-manage bargaining, but the stickiness mechanism itself is Calvo on p. 871.

Taxes appear only as lump-sum taxes in the household and government budget constraints on pp. 910 and 915, with the government generating revenue from lump-sum taxes; there is no distortionary tax rate, tax rule, or tax shock that independently changes equilibrium allocations.

The model includes one-period nominal government debt in the household budget constraint on p. 910 and the government budget constraint on p. 914, but it is only a Ricardian financing device for lump-sum taxes/benefits/spending, not a debt channel that independently affects equilibrium allocations.

Price indexation applies only to non-reoptimizing wholesale firms and is partial: non-reset prices are adjusted by a combination of lagged inflation and steady-state inflation with an indexation parameter x_p in [0,1], not full indexation; see p. 912.
Wage indexation applies only to wage contracts that are not renegotiated, and the indexation parameter x_w allows only partial adjustment to inflation rather than full wage updating; see p. 913.
The paper states in the abstract and again on p. 922 that the authors estimate the model using Bayesian techniques, with an estimation sample of 1984Q1–2006Q4 reported on p. 924.

The model has nontrivial lagged state variables, including price dispersion evolving as Delta_t = h(Delta_{t-1}, Pi_t) under Calvo pricing and debt/borrowing state variables such as b_{t-1}; the paper notes the NK block is purely forward-looking on p. 21 but the financial/debt state variables are genuinely dynamic. See p. 14 and p. 21.

Sticky prices apply to the continuum of differentiated goods/industries produced by monopolistically competitive suppliers rather than a separate final- or intermediate-goods sector; the paper describes each differentiated good being produced by a monopolistically competitive supplier on p. 5 and discusses Calvo pricing and price dispersion on p. 14.

The uploaded source is BIS Working Paper No. 278 dated March 2009, but the paper was later published as “Credit Frictions and Optimal Monetary Policy” in the Journal of Monetary Economics (Vol. 84, December 2016, pp. 30–65).
The uploaded source is a BIS working-paper version (March 2009), but the paper was later published in the Journal of Monetary Economics (Vol. 84, December 2016, pp. 30–65).

The model has a government sector, but its nontrivial role is issuing liquid government paper and conducting private-asset open-market operations, not resource-absorbing government purchases/spending; p. 826 says government paper provides liquidity and nonconventional policy is open-market operations in private assets.

The model assumes standard DSGE/model-consistent expectations; for the crisis simulation, p. 845 says the model paths are computed conditional on the shock hitting at the beginning of the first period under perfect foresight.

The wage block is Calvo wage rigidity without indexation: the online appendix says non-resetting wages remain fixed and the wage law of motion carries forward the previous nominal wage divided by current inflation, not indexed to lagged inflation; the main text only describes price and wage rigidities on p. 826.

True — 50% (1/2 authors). Under the stricter rule, Ravenna does not count: his pre-publication central-bank roles appear to be visiting scholar/researcher roles, while Walsh does count because he was a Senior Economist at the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco before joining UCSC; the paper’s p. 199 lists both authors’ UCSC and FRBSF affiliations.

Taxes T_t are present only as lump-sum taxes in the household cash/budget equation on p. 202, so they do not enter household FOCs or distort private choices; the nontrivial fiscal object is government purchases G_t, not an explicit tax instrument.

The core sticky-price equilibrium is forward-looking: equations (14)–(15) contain current gaps and expected future variables, not lagged endogenous terms; the paper also simplifies by ignoring capital and habit persistence. The AR(1) shock processes used in simulations are exogenous persistence, not intrinsic lagged equilibrium dynamics. See pp. 201 and 205–206.

The composite consumption good consists of differentiated products produced by monopolistically competitive final goods producers, and the Calvo price-setting problem applies to firm j setting the price of its own good; see pp. 201–203.

The sticky-price block is Calvo without indexation: the Phillips curve is purely forward-looking with pi_t = beta E_t pi_{t+1} + kappa(R^_t + s^_t) and contains no lagged inflation term. See pp. 205–206.

Section 3 estimates a New Keynesian inflation-adjustment equation using GMM and quarterly U.S. data from 1960:Q1–2001:Q1; the optimal-policy exercises then calibrate the rest of the model using those estimates rather than fully estimating the whole DSGE system. See pp. 206–207.
The paper estimates the inflation equation using quarterly U.S. data over the sample 1960:Q1–2001:Q1, so the estimate date range starts in 1960:Q1; see p. 206 and the Table 1 note on p. 207.
The paper estimates the inflation equation using quarterly U.S. data over the sample 1960:Q1–2001:Q1, so the estimate date range ends in 2001:Q1; see p. 206 and the Table 1 note on p. 207.

The model includes constant government spending in the GDP identity, but Rannenberg explicitly assumes G_t = G, so government spending is not an active shock, policy rule, or fiscal block driving equilibrium dynamics; see p. 1581.
Taxes enter only as real lump-sum taxes in the household budget constraint on p. 1572; there is no distortionary tax system, tax rule, or tax shock that materially drives equilibrium dynamics.

Government bonds appear only as one-quarter, risk-free household assets that are perfect substitutes with bank deposits and earn the same interest rate; there is no separate government-debt law of motion, fiscal feedback rule, or portfolio/term-premium channel making public debt nontrivially affect equilibrium. See p. 1572.

The model is a standard DSGE with model-consistent expectations: household utility and firm/bank problems are written with E_t expectations over future variables, and the bank leverage condition is explicitly forward-looking; see pp. 1572 and 1576.

For the baseline/full model, Rannenberg calibrates the baseline using a Smets-Wouters parameterization without nominal wage stickiness and lists Erceg-Henderson-Levin nominal wage stickiness only as an alternative/robustness variant; see p. 1585 (and the crisis-experiment variant on p. 1601).
Non-reoptimizing retailers index prices to a weighted combination of lagged inflation and steady-state inflation, so indexation is not full lagged-inflation coverage; see p. 1573.

The model has lump-sum taxes/subsidies and policy instruments, but no government-spending block: the final-goods market clearing condition is y_t = c_t + (phi/2)(pi_t - 1)^2 y_t, so output is absorbed by private consumption and price-adjustment costs, not G_t, on p. 326.

The model is solved with forward-looking model-consistent expectations: the optimal policy problem uses E_t V_{t+1}, and future variables are linear functions of the states with coefficients to be determined, which is a rational-expectations DSGE solution concept on p. 333.

The financial block is state-dependent: bank net worth is inherited from lagged loans and deposits (N_{jt} = R_{t-1} B_{jt-1} - R_{t-1}(1 - S_{t-1}) A_{jt-1}), so past balance-sheet positions affect current leverage/spreads and equilibrium outcomes; see p. 326.

The model has a representative household choosing labor supply with a standard labor-supply FOC on p. 324, while nominal rigidity is introduced through Rotemberg price adjustment costs for final-goods firms on p. 326; there is no wage-setting block, wage Calvo friction, wage adjustment cost, or wage Phillips curve.

All Sectors. RW97 has a single differentiated-goods production structure: each household/producer supplies one differentiated product, and price rigidity applies to those producers generally rather than to a separate final- or intermediate-goods tier. See pp. 308–309 and p. 316.

N/A. RW97 has no sticky wages, so there is no sticky-wage method to classify; the nominal rigidity is price stickiness for differentiated goods producers, not wage contracting/bargaining. See pp. 308–310 and 315–316.

N/A. RW97 has no price indexation, so there is no indexation method to classify; non-adjusting firms keep their nominal prices fixed rather than updating them by lagged or steady-state inflation. See pp. 315–316.

N/A — neither partial nor full. RW97 has no price indexation; firms that cannot reoptimize keep their nominal prices fixed, so there is no indexed subset or universal indexation coverage to classify. See pp. 315–316.
N/A. RW97 has no wage indexation, because it has no sticky-wage block; the only nominal rigidity is Calvo-style price rigidity for differentiated goods producers. See pp. 308–310 and 315–316.

N/A — neither partial nor full. RW97 has no wage indexation because wages are not a separate sticky contracting margin in the model; the nominal rigidity is price rigidity for differentiated goods producers. See pp. 308–310 and 315–316.

January 1997. NBER lists the chapter as published in NBER Macroeconomics Annual 1997, Volume 12 with a Published Date: January 1997; the uploaded paper begins on p. 297. ([nber.org][1]) "An Optimization-Based Econometric Framework for the ..."
False. RW97 was published in NBER Macroeconomics Annual 1997, a conference annual/book-series outlet rather than an academic journal; the paper itself begins as a chapter/article on p. 297.

True. RW97 estimates the monetary-policy VAR/policy rule and chooses structural parameters so the model’s impulse responses match the estimated response of output and inflation to monetary policy shocks. See pp. 299 and 317–318.
1980:Q1. The estimated VAR sample is listed as “SAMPLE: 1980:1–1995:2” in Table 1 on p. 305.
1995:Q2. The estimated VAR sample is listed as “SAMPLE: 1980:1–1995:2” in Table 1 on p. 305.

False (0%). RW97 lists two authors, Julio J. Rotemberg and Michael Woodford, on p. 297. Rotemberg’s pre-1997 record was MIT Sloan faculty from 1980–1996 and Harvard from 1997, while Woodford’s pre-publication central-bank roles were only “Visitor” or “Consultant,” which you exclude. True (50%). The paper has two authors, Emi Nakamura and Jón Steinsson, listed on p. 753; Jón Steinsson’s CV lists Economist, Central Bank of Iceland, 2000–2001, before the March 2014 publication, while Nakamura’s CV lists only a Junior Resident Scholar role at the New York Fed, which I would not count under your “no visiting/scholar positions” rule. True. The model is solved as a standard rational-expectations DSGE: they log-linearize the equilibrium conditions and use Sims (2001), “Solving Linear Rational Expectations Models,” to compute the unique bounded equilibrium. See p. 775.

True. The model has nontrivial lagged/state dynamics: the monetary-policy rule uses interest-rate smoothing with (\rho = 0.8), and government spending shocks follow an AR(1) process with quarterly persistence (0.933). See p. 777.

Final Goods Firms. Sticky prices apply to the differentiated goods-producing firms whose goods are demanded directly by home consumers, foreign consumers, and the government; the paper does not introduce a separate intermediate-goods sector. See p. 774 for the firm sector and p. 775 for the sticky-price calibration.

Not applicable / None. The model does not have sticky wages: wages are determined by the household labor-supply condition, while the only nominal rigidity specified is firms’ price stickiness via (\alpha=0.75) Calvo price setting on p. 775.

Not applicable / None. The model does not have price indexation; sticky prices are modeled with Calvo non-reoptimization probability (\alpha=0.75), with no rule indexing non-reset prices to past, target, or steady-state inflation. See p. 775.

Neither / Not applicable. Price indexation is absent, so there is no partial-versus-full coverage to code; the model uses Calvo price rigidity with (\alpha=0.75), not mechanical indexation of non-reset prices. See p. 775.
Not applicable / None. The model has no wage indexation because it has no sticky-wage block; the only nominal rigidity specified is Calvo price stickiness. See pp. 771 and 775.
Neither / Not applicable. Wage indexation is absent because the model has no sticky-wage or wage-indexation block; only Calvo price stickiness is specified. See pp. 771 and 775.
2014. The model was published in the March 2014 American Economic Review, Vol. 104, No. 3, pp. 753–792; the title page gives “American Economic Review 2014, 104(3): 753–792” on p. 753.

Not applicable. The theoretical model is not structurally estimated, so there is no model estimate date-range start; the empirical regional multiplier exercise uses 1966–2006 data, but that is not an estimation window for the DSGE model. See the empirical-data discussion on p. 758 and the calibrated model setup on p. 777.

Not applicable. The theoretical model is not structurally estimated, so there is no model estimate date-range end; the empirical regional multiplier exercise uses data ending in 2006, but that is not the DSGE model’s estimation range. See empirical sample discussion on p. 758 and calibrated model setup on p. 777.

True (33.3%). The paper has three authors—Monacelli, Perotti, and Trigari—on the title page of the April 2010 NBER version. I would count Antonella Trigari only: her CV lists “Summer 2001 ECB, Research Department, Frankfurt, Graduate Research Programme,” which predates publication and is not listed as a consultancy or visiting position; Monacelli’s and Perotti’s central-bank entries are consultancies/visits, which you told me not to count. True. The model uses standard model-consistent expectations: firms maximize expected discounted profits with (E_t{\cdot}), households maximize expected lifetime utility, and the stochastic discount factor is derived from household optimization. See the baseline model on pp. 10–13 of the NBER version.

Other. Sticky prices are located in a monopolistically competitive retail sector: the search-friction production sector is re-labeled the intermediate-goods sector, while retailers buy intermediate goods, differentiate them, and resell them; the paper also notes final-good firms aggregate individual retail goods. See pp. 31–32 of the NBER version.

Other. It is not Calvo/Rotemberg wage setting or explicit wage contracting in their model; they impose a reduced-form real wage partial-adjustment rule where the actual wage is a weighted average of the Nash-bargained target wage and last period’s actual wage. See p. 29 of the NBER version.

False. The NK extension introduces nominal price rigidity through staggered/Calvo price setting in the retail sector, but the paper does not specify any rule indexing non-reset prices to lagged or steady-state inflation. See pp. 31–33 of the NBER version.

Not applicable. Since the model has no price indexation, there is no indexation method to classify; the NK extension uses Calvo price setting without specifying non-reset-price indexation. See pp. 31–33 of the NBER version.

Partial. More precisely, coverage is none/not applicable, because the model has Calvo price setting but no price-indexation rule for non-resetting firms; if forced into partial vs. full, code Partial, not full. See pp. 31–33 of the NBER version.

Not applicable. The model has no wage indexation; the real wage rigidity extension instead uses lagged real wage levels via (w_t=(1-\chi)w_t^{nb}+\chi w_{t-1}), which is wage partial adjustment, not wage-indexation. See p. 29 of the NBER version.

Partial. More precisely, coverage is none/not applicable, because the model has no wage-indexation rule; it only has real-wage partial adjustment to lagged wage levels. If forced into partial vs. full, code Partial, not full. See p. 29 of the NBER version.

2010. The uploaded version is the NBER working paper dated April 2010 on the title page, and the model is listed as Monacelli, Perotti, Trigari (2010), Journal of Monetary Economics 57, 531–553 in the MMB documentation.
N/A for the theoretical model. The DSGE/search model is calibrated rather than structurally estimated; the paper’s empirical VAR sample starts at 1954:1, stated on p. 4 of the NBER version.
N/A for the theoretical model. The model is calibrated rather than structurally estimated; the paper’s empirical VAR sample ends at 2006:4, stated on p. 4 of the NBER version.

True (100%). Meh is listed on p. 555 as affiliated with the Bank of Canada, and Moran’s CV lists Bank of Canada employment before publication, including Economist in 1992–1993 and Principal Researcher in 1999–2004.

True. The model uses model-consistent forward-looking expectations, with (E_t) entering the household Euler equations and wage-setting first-order condition on pp. 561–562; there is no learning or adaptive-expectations block.

Calvo. Intermediate-good firms face a random price-reoptimization friction: the paper calibrates “the probability of not reoptimizing for price setters” (f_p=0.60), which is the Calvo pricing structure; see p. 565.

Intermediate Goods Firms. The model states that “intermediate goods are produced by monopolistically competitive firms facing nominal rigidities,” while final-good firms are competitive assemblers of intermediate goods; see p. 557.

Calvo. Wage setters receive a random reoptimization signal with probability (1-f_w), otherwise they index wages to last period’s price inflation; the calibration sets (f_w=0.64), the probability of not reoptimizing, on pp. 562 and 565.
Prev Price Inflation. Non-reoptimizing firms index their prices to last period’s aggregate inflation, (p_{j,t+k}=\prod_{s=0}^{k-1}\pi_{t+s}p_{jt}); see p. 558.
Partial coverage. Only firms that do not reoptimize index their prices to lagged aggregate inflation, while the (1-f_p) reoptimizing firms choose a new optimal price; see p. 558.

Partial coverage. Only households that do not reoptimize their nominal wage index it to lagged price inflation; the (1-f_w) reoptimizing households choose a new optimal wage, so indexation does not cover all wage setters; see p. 562.
2010. The paper appears in *Journal of Economic Dynamics & Control* 34 (2010), pp. 555–576; see p. 555.

False (0%). The paper has one author, Pascal Michaillat, listed on p. 190, and his official CV lists only academic employment before the 2014 publication—LSE Lecturer from 2010–2016—with no central-bank employment entry. ([Pascal Michaillat][1]) "Pascal Michaillat's CV"

True. Government spending enters nontrivially through public employment: the government employs workers directly, aggregate employment is (n_t=l_t+g_t), and higher (g) shifts aggregate labor demand (n^d(\theta,w,g)=g+l^d(\theta,w)), affecting tightness, private crowd-out, and equilibrium employment on pp. 192 and 194–195.

False. Although the New Keynesian version writes a government bond/budget equation with inherited debt (b_{t-1}) and new debt (b_t), debt is consolidated out into the resource constraint, which contains consumption, price-adjustment costs, and hiring costs—not a debt-stock mechanism. On p. 204, the paper explicitly rewrites the government budget constraint as the resource constraint, so debt is not a nontrivial equilibrium channel for the model’s multiplier mechanism.

True. The dynamic New Keynesian version uses model-consistent expectations: the firm hiring condition and Phillips curve explicitly contain ( \mathbb{E}_t[\cdot] ) terms, and the simulations are obtained under perfect foresight, which is the deterministic counterpart of rational expectations. See p. 205.

True. Lagged terms matter through the employment law of motion: unemployment at (t) depends on lagged aggregate employment (n_{t-1}), and hiring costs depend on new hires relative to surviving matches; in the NK extension, the resource constraint also contains (n_t-(1-s)n_{t-1}). See pp. 192 and 204.
Rotemberg. The paper states that in the New Keynesian extension, firms face Rotemberg quadratic price adjustment costs, not Calvo price setting. See p. 200.

Intermediate Goods Firms. The final-good market is perfectly competitive, while each intermediate good is produced by a monopolist that incurs Rotemberg price-adjustment costs when changing its nominal price. See p. 204.

Other. Michaillat assumes the real wage is exogenous, following Hall-style real wage rigidity, explicitly not a Calvo/Rotemberg wage-setting or bargaining rule; he notes Nash bargaining is the common alternative but does not use it here. See p. 195.

Not applicable. The model has no price indexation, so none of the listed methods applies; the price friction is Rotemberg quadratic price adjustment costs on p. 200, with no lagged- or steady-state-inflation indexing term.
Neither / not applicable. The model has no price indexation, so there is no partial-versus-full coverage to classify; the price friction is Rotemberg quadratic price adjustment costs on p. 200.

Not applicable. The model has no wage indexation; its wage rigidity is an exogenous real wage schedule rather than indexation to past price inflation, past wage inflation, steady-state inflation, or previous wages. See p. 195.
Neither / not applicable. The model has no wage indexation, so there is no partial-versus-full coverage to classify; wages are treated through an exogenous real wage schedule on p. 195.
2014. The article was published in American Economic Journal: Macroeconomics, 2014, Vol. 6, No. 1, pp. 190–217; the title page shows this on p. 190.
Not applicable. The model was not estimated; it was calibrated to U.S. data, so there is no estimation sample start date. See p. 206.
Not applicable. The model was not estimated; it was calibrated to U.S. data, so there is no estimation sample end date. See p. 206.

False. Government only appears passively through household transfers/bonds in the budget constraint; the equilibrium model is an aggregate demand/supply monetary-policy-rule setup, with no government-purchases shock or fiscal-spending block affecting goods-market equilibrium. See pp. 16, 20–22.

True. The model is explicitly forward-looking/RE: it is built from “rational optimizing behavior” by private agents on p. 16, and the P-bar variant is solved using an MSV/bubble-free rational-expectations solution on pp. 25–26.

True. The core Calvo-Rotemberg private-sector block is “entirely forward looking,” but the implemented model/policy block has nontrivial lagged terms: the estimated policy rule includes (R_{t-1}), and the paper says this implies “a high degree of interest rate smoothing.” See pp. 31–32.

Other. McCallum–Nelson use two price-adjustment variants: a Roberts formulation “based on” Calvo, Rotemberg, and Taylor, plus a modified P-bar model, so the model is not cleanly a single Calvo or Rotemberg setup. See p. 16.

Other. Sticky prices apply to the model’s monopolistically competitive household-producers/suppliers of differentiated goods, not to a separately named final-goods or intermediate-goods sector; the paper says each household “acts as a producer of a good, over which it has market power.” See p. 20.

Not applicable. The model has no sticky wage mechanism: labor supply is effectively inelastic, and the paper notes that current-period wages are irrelevant for current-period employment, so none of Calvo, Rotemberg, wage contracting, or bargaining applies. See pp. 20 and 28 n.21.

Not applicable. There is no price indexation: the Calvo-Rotemberg Phillips curve is forward-looking with no lagged inflation term, and the P-bar alternative is partial price-level adjustment rather than indexation. See pp. 48 and 52.

Not applicable — neither partial nor full. The model has no price indexation term: the Calvo-Rotemberg Phillips curve is purely forward-looking, and the P-bar alternative is price-level adjustment rather than indexation. See pp. 48 and 52.
